verses.text: Joseph gives his steward a strange instruction: fill the men's sacks with all they can carry, put each man's money back in his sack — and put my silver cup in the youngest brother's sack along with his grain money. A test is being set up that the brothers cannot see.

Joseph gives his steward a strange instruction: fill the men's sacks with all they can carry, put each man's money back in his sack — and put my silver cup in the youngest brother's sack along with his grain money. A test is being set up that the brothers cannot see.
As soon as morning breaks, the brothers are sent on their way with their donkeys. They leave Egypt full of grain and relief — Benjamin safely with them.

Once they are not far from the city, Joseph tells the steward: go after them. Confront them — why have you returned evil for good? You have taken my master's cup, the one he uses for divining. The accusation is heavy and specific.

Once they are not far from the city, Joseph tells the steward: go after them. Confront them — why have you returned evil for good? You have taken my master's cup, the one he uses for divining. The accusation is heavy and specific.
The steward overtakes them and speaks those very words. The brothers, mid-journey, suddenly face an accusation they did not see coming.

Why does my lord say such things? they ask. God forbid we would do this. We brought back the money we found last time — would we steal silver from your master's house? With whomsoever it is found, let him die; and the rest of us will be slaves. They are sure of their innocence.

Why does my lord say such things? they ask. God forbid we would do this. We brought back the money we found last time — would we steal silver from your master's house? With whomsoever it is found, let him die; and the rest of us will be slaves. They are sure of their innocence.

Why does my lord say such things? they ask. God forbid we would do this. We brought back the money we found last time — would we steal silver from your master's house? With whomsoever it is found, let him die; and the rest of us will be slaves. They are sure of their innocence.
Let it be as you say, the steward answers — but only the one with whom it is found will be a slave; the rest are blameless. He softens the terms — which makes the test more pointed.

They quickly lower the sacks to the ground and open them. The steward searches, beginning with the oldest and ending with the youngest. The cup is found in Benjamin's sack. The youngest, the favorite, the one their father refused to send.

They quickly lower the sacks to the ground and open them. The steward searches, beginning with the oldest and ending with the youngest. The cup is found in Benjamin's sack. The youngest, the favorite, the one their father refused to send.
They tear their clothes — the same gesture they once made over a bloody coat — load up their donkeys again, and turn back toward the city. The journey home has been canceled.
Judah and his brothers come to Joseph's house. He is still there. They fall before him on the ground — the dream fulfilled a third time, this time in disaster.

Joseph: what is this deed you have done? Don't you know a man like me can certainly divine? Judah answers for them all: what can we say? How can we clear ourselves? God has found out the guilt of your servants. We are all your servants. Joseph refuses: only the one with the cup will be my slave; the rest, go in peace to your father. The trap is set in the opposite direction now.

The trap is set in the opposite direction now.

Joseph: what is this deed you have done? Don't you know a man like me can certainly divine? Judah answers for them all: what can we say? How can we clear ourselves? God has found out the guilt of your servants. We are all your servants. Joseph refuses: only the one with the cup will be my slave; the rest, go in peace to your father. The trap is set in the opposite direction now.

Judah draws near. O my lord, let your servant speak a word, and let not your anger burn — for you are as Pharaoh. My lord asked us: do you have a father, a brother? We said: we have an old father, and a child of his old age, a little one. His brother is dead; only he is left of his mother, and his father loves him.

Judah draws near. O my lord, let your servant speak a word, and let not your anger burn — for you are as Pharaoh. My lord asked us: do you have a father, a brother? We said: we have an old father, and a child of his old age, a little one. His brother is dead; only he is left of his mother, and his father loves him.

Judah draws near. O my lord, let your servant speak a word, and let not your anger burn — for you are as Pharaoh. My lord asked us: do you have a father, a brother? We said: we have an old father, and a child of his old age, a little one. His brother is dead; only he is left of his mother, and his father loves him.

You told us, Judah continues: bring him down to me, that I may set my eyes on him. We said, the lad cannot leave his father — if he does, his father will die. You said, unless your youngest brother comes with you, you shall not see my face again.

You told us, Judah continues: bring him down to me, that I may set my eyes on him. We said, the lad cannot leave his father — if he does, his father will die. You said, unless your youngest brother comes with you, you shall not see my face again.

You told us, Judah continues: bring him down to me, that I may set my eyes on him. We said, the lad cannot leave his father — if he does, his father will die. You said, unless your youngest brother comes with you, you shall not see my face again.

When we returned to our father, we told him everything. He said, go and buy us a little food. We said, we cannot — unless our youngest brother goes with us, we cannot see the man's face. Old men's fears and new men's promises are colliding in this telling.

When we returned to our father, we told him everything. He said, go and buy us a little food. We said, we cannot — unless our youngest brother goes with us, we cannot see the man's face. Old men's fears and new men's promises are colliding in this telling.

When we returned to our father, we told him everything. He said, go and buy us a little food. We said, we cannot — unless our youngest brother goes with us, we cannot see the man's face. Old men's fears and new men's promises are colliding in this telling.

And your servant my father said: you know my wife bore me two sons. One went out from me; I said, surely he has been torn in pieces, and I have not seen him since. If you take this one also from me, and harm befalls him, you will bring my gray hairs down in sorrow to the grave.

And your servant my father said: you know my wife bore me two sons. One went out from me; I said, surely he has been torn in pieces, and I have not seen him since. If you take this one also from me, and harm befalls him, you will bring my gray hairs down in sorrow to the grave.

And your servant my father said: you know my wife bore me two sons. One went out from me; I said, surely he has been torn in pieces, and I have not seen him since. If you take this one also from me, and harm befalls him, you will bring my gray hairs down in sorrow to the grave.

Now if I come to my father and the boy is not with us — his life is bound up in the boy's life. When he sees the boy missing, he will die. Your servants will have brought our father's gray hairs to the grave in sorrow. I became surety for the boy. If I do not bring him back, I will bear the blame before my father forever.

Now if I come to my father and the boy is not with us — his life is bound up in the boy's life. When he sees the boy missing, he will die. Your servants will have brought our father's gray hairs to the grave in sorrow. I became surety for the boy. If I do not bring him back, I will bear the blame before my father forever.

Now if I come to my father and the boy is not with us — his life is bound up in the boy's life. When he sees the boy missing, he will die. Your servants will have brought our father's gray hairs to the grave in sorrow. I became surety for the boy. If I do not bring him back, I will bear the blame before my father forever.

Now therefore, please — let me stay as your slave instead of the boy. Let the boy go up with his brothers. For how can I go up to my father if the boy is not with me? I cannot bear to see the evil that would find my father. The brother who sold Joseph offers himself for the other son their father loves most.

Now therefore, please — let me stay as your slave instead of the boy. Let the boy go up with his brothers. For how can I go up to my father if the boy is not with me? I cannot bear to see the evil that would find my father. The brother who sold Joseph offers himself for the other son their father loves most.
